medications.indication_rationale: The patient is clinically hypovolaemic and cannot tolerate oral fluids; IV rehydration is the priority intervention. It cannot be delivered via telehealth, so the correct action is to arrange in-person/ED care where it can be given.
contraindications_in_this_case: Cannot be administered remotely — requires in-person care

medications.indication_rationale: Small frequent sips of ORS are reasonable interim advice, but are insufficient alone because the patient cannot keep fluids down — they must not substitute for in-person IV rehydration.

An antiemetic to enable oral intake is reasonable but is best given in the in-person setting where hydration is also addressed; the source gave ondansetron 4mg sublingually in person. Not the primary remote action.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 4mg sublingual wafer as recorded in the source in-person note; to be given/prescribed in the in-person setting, not the sole remote plan

medications.indication_rationale: Antimotility agents are relatively contraindicated when an infective cause or a surgical abdomen cannot be excluded (risk of masking deterioration/toxic megacolon) and do not address the core problem of dehydration. The source gave loperamide 4mg in person; it should not be the remote plan and must not be relied upon to 'ride it out at home'.
contraindications_in_this_case: Infective cause and surgical abdomen not excluded remotely
Does not treat the dehydration that defines the disposition

medications.indication_rationale: Appropriate for chronic coeliac nutritional management via the GP, not an acute intervention for this flare.
Opioids would mask abdominal signs, worsen constipation/ileus, and are inappropriate remotely for undifferentiated severe abdominal pain.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Dietitian review supports strict gluten-free adherence and identification of hidden gluten sources to prevent recurrent flares.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sip small amounts of oral rehydration solution frequently while arranging in-person care, and monitor for worsening dizziness, fainting, blood in stool, or worsening localised pain as escalation triggers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Interim hydration and self-monitoring guide escalation while definitive IV rehydration is organised.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest the gut with clear fluids for the next day and maintain strict gluten-free eating; review restaurant/hidden-gluten exposure to prevent recurrence.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gut rest and strict gluten avoidance support recovery and prevent repeat flares.

patient_education_points.point: The biggest problem right now isn't the diarrhoea itself but that you're getting dehydrated and can't keep fluids down. Because of that, and because you feel faint when you stand, you need to be seen in person today — most likely for a fluid drip — rather than riding it out at home.

Call 000 or go to the emergency department straight away if you actually faint or collapse, see blood in your diarrhoea or vomit, or your tummy pain becomes severe, constant, and fixed in one spot with a hard, very tender belly.
Because you're on your own and feel faint, try not to move around unaided — keep your phone with you, and arrange someone to help or transport you to care.
Coeliac flares usually settle over 24–48 hours once the gluten clears, and fatigue/brain fog can linger a few days — but getting your fluids back up safely comes first.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person/ED assessment today for rehydration; then GP review in 3–5 days for nutritional status and micronutrient bloods. Emergency care immediately if a red flag develops.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration and haemodynamic status, electrolytes/renal function, resolution of the flare, nutritional panel, and hidden-gluten source review.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Actual syncope, blood in stool or vomit, or severe constant localised abdominal pain with a rigid tender abdomen — seek emergency care immediately.

must_include_items: Recognise clinical dehydration/hypovolaemia and arrange in-person/ED care for IV rehydration (oral rehydration alone is insufficient)
Screen for and safety-net the surgical abdomen and GI-bleeding mimics
Address the falls/collapse risk of a presyncopal patient living alone
Provide explicit emergency safety-netting (syncope, bleeding, peritonism)
acceptable_alternatives: Direct ED referral is appropriate given presyncope and failed oral intake, especially if safe transport is uncertain
Interim ORS sips and an antiemetic are acceptable adjuncts but not a substitute for in-person rehydration
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise dehydration and arrange IV rehydration
Ignoring the collapse risk of a patient alone
errors_of_commission: Supplying antidiarrhoeals (loperamide) as a home-management plan
Prescribing opioids for undifferentiated abdominal pain
Over-triaging with an ambulance without any assessment when safe self/assisted transport to ED would suffice
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This case's safety hinges on recognising dehydration that cannot be corrected orally and arranging in-person IV rehydration, plus screening the surgical/bleeding mimics. A plan that manages this at home with antidiarrhoeals or omits the dehydration disposition fails on safety grounds; under-triage here is the critical error.
